summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier Tablet speculation with four focused predictions. He argues the tablet will use a closed App Store model identical to the iPhone, may launch without a third-party SDK, could open iTunes content to indie publishers, and almost certainly won't support Flash. His reasoning centers on Apple's desire for control over both software quality and platform economics, and he frames Flash exclusion as an asymmetric bet where Apple risks less by omitting it than by including it.
key_points: The tablet will use a closed App Store as the sole avenue for native third-party apps, because Apple views the iPhone model as a massive success worth replicating
Apple may ship the tablet without an SDK initially, reprising the iPhone's delayed developer story, though the backlash would be louder the second time
An iTunes content store open to indie publishers could disrupt Amazon's Kindle self-publishing model by offering a more favorable revenue split
Flash will almost certainly be excluded because Apple refuses to cede control over core platform software to Adobe, and Flash is the leading cause of crashes on Mac OS X
Omitting Flash is an asymmetric bet: Apple can always add it later, but removing it after launch would be untenable
money_quotes: To me this is so obvious, I didn't consider it worth addressing. I say: yes, of course the App Store will be the only avenue for native third-party tablet apps.

Whatever the problems and complaints developers have about the iPhone App Store, Apple clearly sees it as a huge win. They love the experience it provides, they love the control, and I'm sure they enjoy the 30 percent cut of the revenue.
If you thought there was an uproar with 'just write web apps for it' in 2007, it'd be far louder this time.
Flash is the leading cause of application crashes on Mac OS X. It is buggy. It's inefficient.

Apple controls the entire source code to the iPhone OS. If there's a bug, they can fix it. If something is slow, they can optimize or re-write it. That is not true for Mac OS X, and Flash is a prime example.
The single leading source of application crashes on Mac OS X is a component that Apple can't fix.

If The Tablet ships without Flash and it proves to be a mistake, Apple can always add it later. But if The Tablet does ship with Flash and that proves to be a mistake, it'd be untenable for Apple to subsequently remove it.
The iPhone's utter lack of support for Flash hasn't exactly hurt it.
key_insight: Apple's tablet strategy will prioritize platform control over openness at every level — App Store, SDK, content, and especially Flash — because maintaining full ownership of the software stack is both a competitive advantage and an asymmetric bet.
